I've figured out what is happening. It's a serialization issue. The day of the month is being interpreted as the year, and the year as the day of the month. Maybe Yahoo! changed something. Continuing from above example:

    >>> from pandas_datareader.data import Options
    >>> tsla = Options('tsla', 'yahoo')
    >>> data = tsla.get_all_data()
    >>> data.index.levels[1]
    DatetimeIndex(['2008-04-16', '2015-04-16', '2016-09-16', '2017-06-16',
               '2019-01-18', '2020-01-17', '2020-05-16', '2022-04-16',
               '2029-04-16'],
              dtype='datetime64[ns]', name='Expiry', freq=None)
    >>> data.index.levels[1][0]
    Timestamp('2008-04-16 00:00:00')
    >>> data.index.levels[1][0].to_datetime()
    datetime.datetime(2008, 4, 16, 0, 0)

Note that 2008-04-16 is not a valid expiry at the moment. All expiries for currently available options are in the future. But if we interpret the last 2 digits of the year as the day and the day given above as the year, we get a list of currently valid expiries. The days are always 16, 17 or 18. this is a tricky one as it keeps confusing my brain with several encoding/escaping layers from python and n3 itself... py2.7 vs. 3.4 doesn't seem to be part of the problem here, which is why i'll just use py2.7.

To start off let me just put what i think you want in a `foo.n3` file:
```
<foo:s> <foo:p> "Sample string with trailing backslash\\"^^<http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#string> .
```

As you can see the n3 representation already needs to escape the `\` as otherwise it would itself escape the following `"`.

With that file we can do this:

```python
In [1]: import rdflib
INFO:rdflib:RDFLib Version: 4.2.1

In [2]: g = rdflib.Graph()

In [3]: g.parse('foo.n3', format='n3')
Out[3]: <Graph identifier=Nb7a7399152c14612a6443bdb3c96453d (<class 'rdflib.graph.Graph'>)>

In [4]: list(g)
Out[4]:
[(rdflib.term.URIRef(u'foo:s'),
  rdflib.term.URIRef(u'foo:p'),
  rdflib.term.Literal(u'Sample string with trailing backslash\\', datatype=rdflib.term.URIRef(u'http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#string')))]

In [5]: lit = list(g)[0][2]

In [6]: lit
Out[6]: rdflib.term.Literal(u'Sample string with trailing backslash\\', datatype=rdflib.term.URIRef(u'http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#string'))

In [7]: lit.n3()
Out[7]: u'"Sample string with trailing backslash\\\\"^^<http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#string>'

In [8]: print lit
Sample string with trailing backslash\

In [9]: print lit.n3()
"Sample string with trailing backslash\\"^^<http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#string>
```

Actually line 7 is the most interesting one, as it shows that to represent the n3 string in python we need to double escape it. I kind of abuse `print` here to get rid of the python quoting & escaping layer and show what arrives at n3 / the end-user.

Now your version used `"`, let's explore:
```python
In [1]: sample = "\"Sample string with trailing backslash\\\"^^xsd:string"

In [2]: sample
Out[2]: '"Sample string with trailing backslash\\"^^xsd:string'

In [3]: print sample
"Sample string with trailing backslash\"^^xsd:string
```

The last one actually shows one of the problems: your version lacks another `\` which needs to be doubled to account for the python layer, so you need 5 `\` if you want to use `"` quotes:
```python
In [4]: sample = "\"Sample string with trailing backslash\\\\\"^^xsd:string"

In [5]: sample
Out[5]: '"Sample string with trailing backslash\\\\"^^xsd:string'
```

Why "one of the problems"? Well, cause it still doesn't work :dancer: :facepunch: :
```python
In [6]: from rdflib.util import from_n3
INFO:rdflib:RDFLib Version: 4.2.1

In [7]: from_n3(sample)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
UnicodeDecodeError                        Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-7-2ac3b89358bc> in <module>()
----> 1 from_n3(sample)

/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/rdflib/util.pyc in from_n3(s, default, backend, nsm)
    181         # Hack: this should correctly handle strings with either native unicode
    182         # characters, or \u1234 unicode escapes.
--> 183         value = value.encode("raw-unicode-escape").decode("unicode-escape")
    184         return Literal(value, language, datatype)
    185     elif s == 'true' or s == 'false':

UnicodeDecodeError: 'unicodeescape' codec can't decode byte 0x5c in position 37: \ at end of string```
```


Great, and that's where it now becomes fishy... `rdflib.util.from_n3` is a utility method trying to give us an `rdflib.term` representation of a single `n3` term. It uses a completely different code-base (< 100 lines) than the full `n3` parser (> 1900 lines). My current guess is that one of 3a62086bb08bb7e083fe22778eb2da7b773f451e and 8ea987e8f811128544dff87bf3f8fb4def8f6b4a accounting for (3b1084908abaa0ef0af22d901979bcfecde84247 in `Literal.n3()`) introduced a bug by replacing `\\\\` with `\\` before `.decode('unicode-escape')` does the same. So it was IMO erroneously double-reduced:
```python
In [1]: 'foo\\\\bar'.encode('raw-unicode-escape')
Out[1]: 'foo\\\\bar'

In [2]: 'foo\\\\bar'.encode('raw-unicode-escape').decode('unicode-escape')
Out[2]: u'foo\\bar'

In [3]: 'foo\\\\bar'.decode('unicode-escape')
Out[3]: u'foo\\bar'

In [4]: 'foo\\bar'.decode('unicode-escape')
Out[4]: u'foo\x08ar'
```

(The last one obviously being wrong.)

I'll make a pull request (up for discussion) to fix that, see #548.

In the old fashioned data-center we are using floating IP. Patroni has callback mechanism, which also seems to be not documented properly.
In the postgresql section of config file it is possible to configure callbacks which would be triggered on some actions:
 - on_start # when the postgres is started
 - on_stop # when the postgres was stopped
 - on_restart # when the postgres was restarted
 - on_reload # when pg_ctl reload was executed
 - on_role_change # when postgres was either promoted to master or demoted to read-only

In the all cases callback is called with 3 arguments: <cb_name> <role> <scope>
 - cb_name - possible values: on_start, on_stop, etc..
 - role - current role of the node (master, replica, demoted, etc...)
 - scope - name of the Patroni cluster

Indeed, this is something we didn't cover until now. I pushed [an update](https://github.com/algolia/algoliasearch-django-example/commits/master) to our demo app to showcase how you can turn off `algoliasearch-django` in your tests:

- Update your `settings.py` to add a setting for when tests are running:
  ```python
  # TESTING: Check if running tests
  import sys
  TESTING = sys.argv[1:2] == ['test']
```

- In your `AppConfig`, only register Algolia when you are not testing:
  ```python
  if not settings.TESTING:
     algoliasearch.register(Contact, ContactIndex)
  ```

With this approach, you won't have algolia registered when running your tests.
This should solve your issue, but if it persists please reopen this ticket and let me know what's happening!

@gamaanderson. Sorry for the confusion. Let me give an example. If a radar is at `(lat_r, lon_r)`, given a beam at azimuth `alpha`. Considering to all beam actually is a great circle, so we get all range gates on that beam presented by (lat, lon) using pyproj by calculating the geodesic distance. We can repeat this operations for all radars in a multi-radar case. But then if a distance-weighted interpolation is needed, we come to the problem that we need reproject (lat, lon) back to in order to get the line distance. I consider this procedure involves too many unnecessary project/inverse project calculations. So I think why not put all radars on LCC, make geodesic convergence corrections on each radar then gridding, interpolating. If a lat-lon grid is desired, we could inverse project them back. If another projection is desired, we project the mosaic to another coordinates. That simplifies calculations. Furthermore, I just feel that LCC is not a bad idea at all.

Is `pyproj` is a dependency of `matplotlib`? I don't think `matplotlib` requires `pyproj` because it is directly based on either proj4 or gdal C codebase (which one I don't remember)

FWIW, the `Topic.publish' wrapper actually adds a timestamp into the message attributes, IFF `timestamp_messages` is set to `True` on the topic:  that is the value which `Message.timestamp` expects to consume.  The need for such a field was discussed in #797, which quoted the pubusb docs as saying:

> Although Pub/Sub usually delivers messages in order of publication, this is not guaranteed; it is possible for subscriptions to receive messages out of order. For this reason, we suggest that you include sequence information in the message payload or attribute so that subscribers that need in-order messaging can implement logic to do so.

Support for generating the attiribute was added to `Topic` in #809, and the property which parses it out was added to `Message` in #815.

If I had been aware of the `PubsubMessage.publishTime` field, I would have just recommended using it, rather than adding an extra attribute.

From a conversations with me @crwilcox offline for records:

> the root cause is that gRPC can mark the RPC as inactive in another thread, after recv() has been called. So background consumer would recv(), get a (buffered) response, and the gRPC would reap the RPC right after. When background consumer would go to the top of its while loop, the `is_active` condition would be `False`. Had anything called `send()` or if the background consumer called `recv()` somehow before then, the RPC would be re-established.

There are *three* changes to address this:

1. we attempt to recover from a place we didn't previously (the gRPC error callback).

> This is defensive. Why not just go ahead and recover in the error callback? In practice, this preempts `recoverable` (which wraps `send` and `recv`) from ever "winning the race" to recover the stream. The stream will always be recovered by the gRPC error callback.

2. we monitor a different semaphore for if a grpc channel is actually active.

> This alone is probably enough to fix the condition above. By checking if the bidi rpc is *finalized* instead of whether or not the underlying RPC is active, the `while` condition in the background consumer would be `True` right up until finalization.

3.  when we can't recover in recoverable, finalize in addition to close. We use if it was finalized to detect if the stream is active.

> This is a follow-up to (2) that makes `is_active` stay semantically correct. Without it, recoverable would never mark the recoverable bidi rpc as inactive in the case of an unrecoverable error.

Using this paradigm, introducing a new function definition requires an update to each distro utils file. Not sure if we should change the design, but we should give it some thought.

We discussed this internally. We think we should keep `utils.py` an interface where each function throws an unimplemented exception instead of doing nothing. This will make it more obvious when new functionality is introduced that isn't getting overridden by one of the distros. It's fine if each distro implements the functionality by calling the helpers function, though we may way to split up helpers at some point if it becomes unwieldy.

Let me know if you have any questions/concerns with this approach.

@ddebrunner: We make every attempt to ensure compatibility across all versions of `dill`.  There's a test suite that is tested on every checkin, and is used to ensure that the results don't change.  I do not like to accept changes that break backward compatibility, and have noted any time that has happened.  It's very very very few times over the course of 10+ years (I can think of only once it was done intentionally).  That being said, we just broke it across versions of `dill` unintentionally.  Mostly because we didn't think of testing the particular case that was broken by the change.  Had we thought of it, we'd have avoided the break (instead of rectifying it with a patch release).  We don't test have a test against different flavors of old pickles. We could, but we don't at the moment. Shadowsocks 是为用户服务的，不是为站长服务的

那一堆的加密实现实在没必要，留两三个最强的就行。
Shadowsocks的TCP转发，接立好连接后相当于三条TCP连接，从应用到本地的ss客户端，从本地ss客户端到服务器上的ss服务，然后从ss服务到真正的目标ip。@shinku721，使用了OTA后TCP发送的每一段数据trunk都有一个index，ss服务每次收到一段合法的trunk后index自增1，所以
1.  在该TCP转发链还没断之前，ss服务收到一个replay的trunk，会因为index不对而丢弃。另外，TCP发数据包有sequance值用于确认，同一TCP连接上已经ack过的sequance按理来说在驱动层就自动过滤掉了吧？不太可能还会返回给回应用层
2.  在该TCP转发链没了之后，ss服务对应的转发socket已经销毁了，找不到转发socket应该也会丢弃replay的Trunk包
3.  实现上TCP每次send一段数据未必就只是一个TCP数据包，所以Replay的话，连真正的Trunk数据包边界都很可能是不对的，这种情况下服务器也不可能返回期待的数据包

所以ss里实现的OTA是能保证TCP不受replay attack的。至于UDP，不会有上述3的问题，但如上述1的情形即转发socket还在时，还是避免不了你说的replay attack探测。不过，以发送同一个数据包就收到特定数据包就认为是个代理，这个判断规则感觉就不准确。

个人觉得OTA，TCP转发的实现上使ss本来简单的协议变很复杂了，而且效率也低了，因为要等待trunk接收完成才能转发。得想一个更简单有效的方法。

题外话：OTA是客户端已经主动设为True了吗，比如ShadowsocksX

至于流量混淆，是很有必要的，如果在原来加密的基础上，把addr type的第7位用上（第8位用于OTA标识了），作为流量混淆的标记，再后一字节作为流量混淆算法，然后流量混淆算法就可以大家百家争鸣了。

是检测到异常流量（比如没法识别特征）后直接封IP。

建议更换IP，一般可以坚持一段时间。

to run a pex'd application in uwsgi, you can globally inject pex bootstrapping code via uWSGI's import (e.g. `--import bootstrap.py`) option. you need some way to pass the path of the target pex(es) to the bootstrapper such as an environment variable etc (tho uwsgi may offer a cleaner alternative to this via one of its many options). from there, you you can simply pass in an application module (e.g. `--module your.import.namespace:app`) and uWSGI will load and run the given app from within the pex.

Totally agree with you @TaoLv and I think its great to have a well-working RNN-type model readily available in Sockeye for others to use! My point was to ask how sure you (or others) are that this configuration is as close as possible to the GNMT model (given that there was a discussion around the correct parameters in #479). I haven't looked into replicating GNMT models in Sockeye, but did for the transformer and there, many details (down to embedding initialization, scaling etc.) are crucial to reproduce published results.
If the config in this PR does equally or better than published results in the GNMT paper, this is awesome! But we should try to be extremely clear on what number in the paper we are comparing against, how we compute it, how - to the best of our knowledge - the setup differs from the published paper, and what language pair/dataset we are reporting this on.
Again, more presets/configs for Sockeye autopilot are super welcome and helpful, but in my experience replicating published models across different toolkits and frameworks in a fair way can be quite complex, and before we make any claim that GNMT models in MXNet/Sockeye do better than previously published results, we need to be absolutely sure about it.

That said, calling the current config `gnmt_like` is absolutely fine with me and you can always iterate on it to make it more precise (maybe to eventually call it `gnmt`).

From my perspective ... AFAIK asyncio is available only for Python 3.3 and higher, because it uses 'yield from' which was introduced first into Python 3.3. That's because Guido van Rossum doesn't like callbacks. (Trollius is deprecated.) 

Apparently, the asyncio event loop is intended to be interoperable with other frameworks (like Twisted), but I couldn't easily do any testing, because I'm on Python 2. 

Looked at from the other perspective, why has Guido van Rossum created asyncio when Twisted etc already exist? I think it's because he feels async has to be addressed better in standard Python. I don't think it's his intention to replace other frameworks or make them redundant. Twisted is a mature framework that does a lot more than provide an event loop.

@rtibbles @66eli77 I'd like to merge this in so that folks can continue to be working off it. However I also want to make sure that any outstanding comments are taken into account eventually. Creating this Trello card as a reminder: https://trello.com/c/3f5ogNYr/307-follow-up-on-resource-persistence-related-pr

Short answer: Python-for-Android converts a numeric period-separated version into the "pure integer" version that Android (and the Play Store) need. This resulting number needs to be monotonically increasing otherwise you can't install on top of an older version, and we want to be able to upgrade within alpha/beta versions, for testing. The scheme here was the best way we could think of to accomplish that. Don't see any usefulness outside of Android.

I will add some details tomorrow -- but in the meantime, best practices are to not do any normalization for ELMo.  allennlp has the ability to maintain separate namespaces for different indexing schemes, so you can have both lowercased tokens for GloVe and unnormalized character based ids for ELMo.  This is exactly what the example in the ELMo tutorial does, see the sections here: https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/master/tutorials/how_to/elmo.md#using-elmo-with-existing-allennlp-models

The `<S>` and `</S>` are added outside the indexers.  They are added inside the ELMo module and then removed, so you will never see them if you treat ELMo as a black box so don't add them to your text (if you are using the provided allennlp indexers and embedders).

It's all through git remotes, so you can do it the following way:

```
$ deis create appname-prod -r prod
$ deis create appname-dev -r dev
$ deis create appname-qa -r qa
```

Then, once that's done you can use `deis apps:info -a appname-dev` to explicitly to state that you want to run this command against the dev version and `git push dev master` to push changes to the `dev` application.

This wouldn't make it into v1.x, as we're not adding new features unless we and the community jointly judge them to be must haves for an LTS.  You could open this issue against deis/router instead.

That said, I would, personally, still be opposed to this as it drastically increases complexity in a number of ways.  For instance, today, most config is managed at the app level.  Apps have domains.  What you're proposing inverts that relationship such that domains have apps.  Not only is that more difficult for someone familiar with the Heroku workflow to wrap their head around, but it would make configuration way more difficult for a Deis user.  You want to increase a timeout somewhere, for instance?  Is that on a domain?  Or on one of the potentially many containers serving that domain?  I'm siding with the simplicity of the current model.

All this being said, you _can_ do this yourself.  The app for such a domain could be your own nginx, for instance, statically configured to proxy requests for `www.example.com/blog` to `blog.example.com` while the rest are proxied to the container for the www app.  I know this adds an extra hop... so if you were feeling ambitious you could customize the deis-router instead to handle this special case with some static config.  Just some ideas.

For `pip install turbodbc` to work
- Visual C++ 2015 update 3 needs to be installed. (pybind11 has a [hard requirement](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/v2.1/include/pybind11/common.h#L54) on update 3. This unfortunately excludes the lighter [Visual C++ 2015 Build Tools](http://landinghub.visualstudio.com/visual-cpp-build-tools)). My guess is that this is somewhat common at least.
- Boost need to be installed, bootstrapped and the environment configured so that turbodbc can find it. I imagine this is very uncommon. (If not bootstrapped linking complains about libboost_date_time. When boost date handling goes away, it might be possible to do a source only build.)

In conclusion, without following the readme almost no one will get this working on Windows. If the goal is smooth install experience, a binary wheel is indeed needed.

> When it is True, we are not giving Conda carte blanche to update everything. Instead, conda updates dependences on an as-needed basis. When a dependency does need to be updated, it is updated as much as possible; but if the current dependency is sufficient to handle the requested package, it is not touched. And according to conda info ipython=4.2.0=py27_1, all of the dependencies currently in your environment at this point were satisfactory.

That's what the `false` value is supposed to do. What does setting it to false actually do? Create inconsistent solutions?

> We went back and forth on this when the new solver was being demonstrated. People were quite concerned that the more aggressive dependency resolution was effectively going to force far too many updates.

I get that you don't want to just install something, and have every conda install basically be `conda update --all`. But I would argue that the dependencies for any package that you request should be updated, and it should only be conservative for the other packages that are installed. 

From what I recall, there's three different "strings" of versions for any given package

- The currently installed version
- All versions newer than the currently installed version (in order)
- All versions older than that version

`update_dependencies: true` orders versions basically ignoring the current version. `update_dependencies: false` orders them as `current version > reversed(newer versions) > older versions` (if I remember correctly; please correct me if I am wrong)

Probably a different order is needed, even for `update_dependencies: true` for the tertiary packages that aren't requested or dependencies of what is requested. (and I know that "dependencies of what is requested" is a shaky term)

I get that people are concerned about conda updating more than they want, but the default options to be to keep stuff updated. That option was added for people who want to have more conservative setups.

> So conda is behaving as expected here. I'd argue this is a bug in ipython's conda recipe.

But in reality, almost no recipe specifies a minimum version. This just work because it picks the latest versions. 

I suspect that biggest issue here is not really this, but the channel priority stuff. That sounds like a real mess. Are you changing what `conda config --add channels` does here as well?

> Summary
>
> Conda features are a great tool to distinguish variants of the same
> package, but manual configuration of features does not scale when this
> capability gets used for a wide variety of properties. This issue proposes
> to apply the existing jinja2 templating mechanism to configure features
> automatically at package build time. Recipes equipped with suitable jinja2
> code can be build with a specific set of properties in three simple steps:
>
>    - Create an environment where the desired features are tracked.
>    - Activate that environment.
>    - Run conda build my-package. It applies jinja2 preprocessing to
>    my-package/meta.yaml, re-creates the current setup in the temporary
>    _build environment and then compiles my-package with the desired
>    features.
>
> The proposed solution does not require changes to conda itself, just
> enhanced recipes. A successful preliminary implementation (which differs
> from the present proposal in some details) can be found in the recipe
> collection
> https://github.com/ilastik/ilastik-build-conda/tree/toolset-config.
> Rationale
>
> One and the same version of a package can exist in several incompatible
> variants. For example, we may compile boost with different compilers (say,
> vc10 and vc14) and link the boost::python library against different Python
> versions (say, 2.7 and 3.5) to end up with four incompatible builds of the
> same boost version. Similarly, we may build numpy with various choices of
> the BLAS library such as Intel MKL or OpenBLAS. Other packages that also
> require BLAS should then choose the same variant as numpy did to ensure
> compatibility.
>
> Conda uses clever conventions to avoid coexistence of incompatible
> variants whenever possible:
>
>    - There is a one-to-one correspondence between Python versions and
>    compiler versions.
>    - Packages are updated in lock-step so that the set of newest versions
>    (at any given time) is compatible.
>
> When this is not possible, conda encodes compatibility information into
> the build string. For example, _np110py27 means that a package requires
> Python 2.7 and numpy 1.10. This approach works reasonably, but is not
> always reliable because conda's version resolution algorithm treats build
> strings as secondary entities: (i) build strings are only looked at when
> version and build numbers are identical; (ii) build string comparison only
> supports exact matching, no relational comparisons or wild cards as in
> version numbers.
>
> Moreover, the above conventions may be too restrictive in certain
> situations: Some users may be forced by external circumstances to use a
> different compiler than conda's default, or may be restricted to a
> particular version of a package so that packages cannot be updated in
> lockstep. Therefore, conda needs a more powerful mechanism to specify
> requirements for compatible variants.
>
> Fortunately, such a mechanism already exists: conda's 'features'
> capability. For example, to ensure that a package is only used in
> combination with packages that were built by the same compiler, a recipe
> would specify:
>
> build:
>   features:
>     - vc10
>
> if the package requires Visual Studio 2010. As a consequence, this
> particular package would only be considered in conda environments that are
> based on this compiler. A conda environment acquires this feature-enforcing
> behavior by installing a package which tracks the feature, i.e. whose
> recipe contains:
>
> build:
>   track_features:
>     - vc10
>
> To make full use of 'features', three questions need to be answered:
>
>    1. Which packages need to enforce (i.e. track) features in order to
>    ensure that only compatible dependents are installed, and what
>    conventions/standards shall be observed to specify those features?
>    2. How can we insert the appropriate 'features' and 'track_features'
>    specifications into the recipes automatically, especially when requirements
>    are transitive?
>    3. How can we guarantee that 'conda install' always respects the
>    feature specifications?
>
> 1) Which features?
>
> As an introductory example, consider the problem of incompatible compilers
> on Windows. Conda already uses the features 'vc10', 'vc14' etc. to
> distinguish compilers on windows. Since each Python version in conda is
> tied to a particular compiler version, the corresponding 'track_features'
> spec is found in the Python recipe. This leads to the slight oddity that
> pure C++ packages must still list Python as a build requirement in order to
> activate the appropriate compiler (Insteadt, it might be better to create a
> dedicated 'visual-studio' package which installs the appropriate runtime
> libaries and activates the appropriate 'track_features'.) More importantly,
> it prevents users from compiling Python with a different compiler. For
> example, it is impossible to build extension modules that require C++11 for
> Python 2.7 on Windows because Visual Studio 2010 (conda's default for
> Python 2.7) only supports C++98. Conversely, one cannot use Python 3.5 when
> one is forced (by whatever reason) to an older compiler than conda's
> default.
>
> At present, this problem is most pronounced on Windows, because Visual
> Studio versions produce mutually incompatible code. In contrast, the gcc
> 4.x compiler series on Linux generally generate compatible binaries.
> However, incompatibilities may arise with the more widespread use of gcc
> 5.x and clang, and by incompatible versions of the C++ standard library
> (libstdc++ vs. libc++ on Mac is a case in point). Moreover, compilers are
> not the only source of incompatibility, as the above example of numpy with
> MKL vs. numpy with OpenBLAS demonstrates. I will look into this example in
> the next section. In conclusion:
>
>    1. Packages where the same version number exists in multiple, binary
>    incompatible variants must define (i.e. track_features) a feature.
>    2. Dependents of such packages must be built in the same variants, and
>    those variants are distinguished by the appropriate features
>    specification.
>    3. Feature names should follow the established conventions of 'vc10'
>    and 'vc14' or 'np100' and 'np110'.
>    4. The set of properties supported by features must be clearly
>    documented in the conda manuals. There should be an well-documented open
>    process to discuss and accept proposed additions to the set.
>
> 2) How to add feature specs to recipes automatically?
>
> At present, conda features are specified manually in a package recipe's
> meta.yaml file, e.g.
>
> build:
>   features:
>     - vc9   # [win and py27]
>     - vc10  # [win and py34]
>     - vc14  # [win and py35]
>
> to select the appropriate compiler runtime according to the Python
> version. However, this approach does not scale to more widespread
> application of the 'features' mechanism, and is not flexible enough to
> allow Python to be compiled with a non-standard compiler.
>
> However, the actual difficulty is more fundamental: meta.yaml are static
> text files by nature, whereas the information about features is only
> available at build time (because it depends on the build environment), and
> may differ between different builds of the same recipe. Conda currently
> solves this by means of conditionals (the [win and py27] above), but this
> technique is severely limited by the restricted set of supported conditions.
>
> But once again, conda already offers a solution: the possibility to
> customize meta.yaml files at build time by means of jinja2 preprocessing.
> A simple meta.yaml that configures the compiler feature at build time
> looks like this:
>
> # import the feature spec of the active compiler
> {% import '$CONDA_ACTIVE_ENV/conda-meta/features/compiler.yaml' as compiler %}
>
> package:
>   name: zlib
>   version: "1.2.8"
>
> build:
>   features:
>     - {{compiler.feature}}  # add feature for the current build
>                             # (expands into 'vc10', 'vc14' etc. as appropriate)
>
> requirements:
>   build:
>     - {{compiler.build_requirement}}  # list the correct compiler as a requirement
>
>   run:
>     - {{compiler.run_requirement}}    # list the compiler runtime as a requirement
>
> source:
>   ... # as usual
> about:
>   ... # as usual
>
> Specifically, for a vc14 (Visual Studio 2015) build, the variables resolve
> into
>
> {{compiler.feature}}            => vc14
> {{compiler.build_requirement}}  => visual-studio-build  14.0
> {{compiler.run_requirement}}    => visual-studio        14.0
>
> The file conda-meta/features/compiler.yaml is installed by the command conda
> install visual-studio-build=14.0 which prepares the current environment
> for conda builds with this compiler. Ideally, this command would also
> install the compiler into the current environment, but since this is not
> permitted by Microsoft's license and would be too time consuming anyway, it
> just checks that the correct compiler is in the PATH, and terminates
> otherwise (e.g. if it finds Visual Studio 10.0 instead). The package
> visual-studio just installs the compiler runtime DLLs. Moreover, both
> packages contain a track-features: specification with the appropriate
> feature tag.
>
> The technique can be easily extended to other sources of variation. For
> example, anaconda currently enforces a one-to-one relationship between
> compilers and Python versions (e.g. Python 2.7 is tied to Visual Studio
> 2008 on Windows). Thus, the compiler feature implies the Python version. If
> anaconda drops this restriction, an additional feature will be needed to
> distinguish between Python versions, e.g. py27 and py35. Another
> interesting case is the BLAS library to be used by numpy, where users may
> want to choose between Atlas, Intel MKL, OpenBLAS and possibly others. Once
> numpy is compiled against a specific BLAS library, all other packages that
> need BLAS should use the same one. This is again best handled by a feature
> that takes values like blas_atlas3_10, blas_mkl11_3, blas_open0_2.
>
> Using features for the compiler, Python version and BLAS variant, the
> numpy recipe's meta.yaml may look like
>
> {% import '$CONDA_ACTIVE_ENV/conda-meta/features/compiler.yaml' as compiler %}
> {% import '$CONDA_ACTIVE_ENV/conda-meta/features/python.yaml'   as python %}
> {% import '$CONDA_ACTIVE_ENV/conda-meta/features/blas.yaml'     as blas %}
>
> package:
>   name: numpy
>   version: "1.10.1"
>
> build:
>   features:
>     - {{compiler.feature}}
>     - {{python.feature}}
>     - {{blas.feature}}
>
> requirements:
>   build:
>     - {{compiler.build_requirement}}
>     - {{python.build_requirement}}
>     - {{blas.build_requirement}}
>     - fftw
>     - cython
>     - setuptools
>
>   run:
>     - {{compiler.run_requirement}}
>     - {{python.run_requirement}}
>     - {{blas.run_requirement}}
>     - fftw
>
> source:
>   ... # as usual
> about:
>   ... # as usual
>
> In summary, to build a package variant aginst a specific collection of
> dependencies, you
>
>    - Create an environment that contains the desired dependencies.
>    Specifically, the required jinja2 template files must be installed under
>    conda-meta/features/.
>    - Activate that environment.
>    - Run conda build pkg-name. It applies jinja2 preprocessing to
>    meta.yaml, re-creates the current setup in the temporary _build
>    environment and then compiles the new package with the desired features.
>
> No changes to conda itself are needed to implement the approach outlined
> above - everything can be achieved by providing suitable recipes.
> Specifically, packages that track features must specify the appropriate
> track_features tag and install a jinja2 template under
> conda-meta/features/. Packages that want to use a feature must read the
> jinja2 template from conda-meta/features/ and apply the appropriate
> configurations in the features and requirements sections of their
> meta.yaml file.
> 3) How to enforce features in 'conda install'?
>
> In theory, conda's version resolution algorithm resolves feature
> requirements automatically, provided the settings have been correctly
> configuered in the package recipes. Unfortunately, the present version
> resolution has a bug that prevents this from working in all situations, see
> issue #1589 <https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1589> and references
> therein for details. It appears to be difficult to fix this bug without
> making version resolution very slow.
>
> A possible work-around is to add feature tags to the version number. For
> example, zlib compiled with Visual Studio 2015 would become
> zlib-1.2.8.vc14. This is similar to conda's current convention to add
> feature tags to the build string, but is much more powerful: it makes all
> the sophistication of conda's version comparison available to feature
> matching as well. It is easy to use this technique to make sure that all
> packages in a given conda environment are built with the same compiler. Foe
> example, when we build the recipe for libtiff with Visual Studio 2015, we
> can specify its dependency on zlib as:
>
> requirements:
>   run:
>     - zlib   *.vc14*
>
> Incompatible zlib variants lack the .vc14 tag in their version number and
> will thus be excluded from version resolution. Hard-wiring a specific tag
> value can again be avoided by means of jinja2 variables:
>
> {% import '$CONDA_ACTIVE_ENV/conda-meta/features/compiler.yaml' as compiler %}
>
> requirements:
>   run:
>     - zlib   *{{compiler.version_tag}}*
>
> Version tags would also simplify the process of setting up a conda
> environment with specific properties. For example, to create an environment
> containing scipy and numpy build against the OpenBLAS library, one could
> simply write
>
> conda create -n scipy-openblas   scipy=0.16.1.blas_open0_2
>
> This command would recursively install openblas and numpy with the desired
> feature.
>
> A potential problem with version tags is that a version number should
> probably only contain at most two or three of them to avoid clutter. Thus,
> issue #1589 <https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1589> still needs to
> be solved. Nonetheless, version tags may remain a useful technique to
> simplify conda installation commands.
>
> Of course, many details need to be discussed and improved before this
> proposal can be made an official part of the conda infrastructure. However,
> a working preliminary implementation (which uses jinja2 templates for just
> the compiler configuration and differs in various details from the present
> text) can be found in the recipe collection
> https://github.com/ilastik/ilastik-build-conda/tree/toolset-config. It
> provides around 130 recipes to compile a Python 2.7 ecosystem with Visual
> Studio 2012 and has worked very well for creating the ilastik interactive
> learning and segmentation toolkit on Windows (see http://ilastik.org).
>
> Please comment!
>
> —
> Reply to this email directly or view it on GitHub
> <https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1959>.

> On Jul 29, 2015, at 4:42 AM, Victor Baybekov <notifications@github.com> wrote:
> 
> Floating point values have very different bits even for close values, so shuffle is of little help. In .NET world I am converting floats to decimals (no loss of precision) and taking diff (no loss of precision for decimals) - then shuffle shines on the diff values. Taking diff directly on floats loses precision and will be useless since the diff values are also floating point and suffer from different bits unless the diff is exactly the same (very rare cases). For real data (e.g. stock prices) I have quite high compression with decimals compared to floats, yet the speed is much slower but still faster than IO - I use Blosc for disk storage and network, not in-memory data transfer. Another point is that for certain data, e.g. financial, you have to store data as decimal even if you use it in analytical calculations as floats - to avoid issues line 0.9-0.6-0.3 <> 0.0, so for me decimal was very good solution.

Based on what you've shared, I think I can help:

> I am running elasticsearch version 5.5.0 on 2 data nodes, 1 master and 1 client node, curator 5.1.1 on amazon aws (ubuntu). our repository is in S3. our active shards is around 5200

This is your problem in a nutshell: **You have heap exhaustion.**  

You have 2 data nodes, with 2600 shards per node.   I don't even have to look at any of the other statistics, or configuration.  Even with a 30G heap size, you shouldn't have more than 1000 shards per node.  If you look at the JVM stats, I am convinced you'll find that your heap is well over 70% full, all the time, and it's trying to do full GCs one right after the other.  This is why your requests are timing out.  The data nodes are out of usable memory, and they are fighting to do what you ask, but it's just taking too long.

If you want things to work properly again, your recourses are as follows:

1. Add more nodes to bring the shard count per node well below 1000 (especially if your data node heap size is < 30g)
1. Delete a ton of indices to bring the shard count per node down to well below 1000.
1. Increase the heap (if even possible), though this won't help much if the shard count is still over 1000 per node.

Let's start with these questions:

How large are your index names? Can you give me a sample of what they look like?

Is there more log data than what you've provided?  You only included the error. Was there more?

What is the full text of your action YAML file? I'd like to see how your indices were being selected and filtered.
It will probably be fixed in Curator 5.0, as I believe it's an artifact of using the 2.x API calls to 5.0.

the error you're getting is a result of a network connection or firewall rule or something. 
It is actually not an error related to 
1. Elasticsearch
2. Elasticsearch-py (this library)

This error is coming from urllib3. And the reason you're getting this error is because python can not make a connection to the HTTP host it's trying to connect to. 

If we take out Elasticsearch and Elasticsearch-py. This error happens because a python process using URLlib3 can not connect to a networked host. 

This is why I asked all the questions, because somewhere you have a network configuration that's causing the python process to not be able to connect to a host in your cluster. It could be related to anything from the VM firewall rules, the host firewall rules, the data center firewall rules, routers, etc..

No, refresh operation returns `Accepted` status code and `Location` header. You need to poll location header to get wait for completion. Refresh api doesn't return any response on completion so you need to wait for `200/201/204` status code. 

To get operation Id, parse location header returned by refresh. It contains operation Id.

Do you mean your yaml file is like:

```
search_rules:
    from-date: 2017-06-01
    to-date: 2017-09-01 01:01
    pt-rule: kanye


search_params:
    results-per-call: 500
    max-results: 500

If that is the case, note that the query operators are a part of a powertrack rule, in a string, rather than the parameters for the search function itself. `search_params` define information about how many results and results-per-api-call are defined. The premium search operators can only be used inside of the `pt-rule` field, as such:

```
search_rules:
    from-date: 2017-06-01
    to-date: 2017-09-01 01:01
    pt-rule: from:vimal
```

Finally, I changed my mind from using `message.finish()` after death of thread to:
- When getting new task, immediately calls `message.finish()` and put task into memory
- Use independent threads cleaning tasks from memory, to get away from pynsq life circle (tournado ioloop).
- Set limitation on memory pool (same amount as `max_in_flight`) avoiding tasks comming like waterfall

Here's the code and it's working fine for now:
```
message_pool = []
lock = threading.Lock()

def consume_func(content):
    # do custom stuff with content
    time.sleep(5)
    return

def thread_consumer():
    while True:
        message_body = None

        lock.acquire()
        global message_pool
        if message_pool:
            message_body = message_pool.pop()
        lock.release()

        if message_body:
            try:
                consume_func(message_body)
            except Exception as e:
                logger.error('异步消费时出错:')
                logger.exception(e)
        else:
            time.sleep(0.1)

def async_handler(message):
    global message_pool
    message.enable_async()

    while True:
        if len(message_pool) < max_threads_in_worker:
            lock.acquire()
            message_pool.append(message.body.decode())
            lock.release()
            message.finish()
            break
        else:
            time.sleep(0.1)

for i in range(max_threads_in_worker):
    job = threading.Thread(target=thread_consumer)
    job.start()

r, nsq = get_nsq_reader(async_handler, 'athena.'+self.listen_queue, 'async', max_threads_in_worker)
nsq.run()
```

What we can do for the Report and Node type is this:

- If `noop` == `false` use that status field value (existing logic)
- If `noop` == `true` and the status field == `unchanged` set the object's status field to `unchanged`
- If `noop` == `true` and the status field != `unchanged` set the object's status field to `noop`

This would be released with 0.3.2 release and would still be backward compatible. Mind you the new `noop_pending` fields in PuppetDB >= 4.2.0 appear to cover this logic but relying strictly on these fields may break compatibility in PuppetDB < 4.2.0. We will maintain compatibility between minor versions and implement the breaking changes between major versions only (if released).

* let's assume WinForms/WPF can incorporate Core/Standard bits. Fair enough; targeting what runtime(s)? Framework 4.6.2, for instance? Could be. But I think what most folks, including myself, perhaps badly informed, are expecting, is a Core/Standard comprehension.

* you are also assuming calls for a WinForms/WPF port into "native" Standard/Core, so to speak, is to the exclusion of other delivery modes, i.e. console applications, services, etc. I cannot speak for others, but that assertion is utterly **FALSE**, on ***EVERY*** level.

* I don't know how you can say that many systems do not have GUI's. GUIs are indeed dominant across most platforms in the desktop world. Even most modern ``*nix`` platforms have **SOME** sort of GUI comprehension, when you take into consideration Linux flavors, Ubuntu, Mint, etc. So, this is not a tall expectation by any means. Even if it is a shim to something like Gtk, Qt, or whatever. Make **SOMETHING** happen toward these ends.

* some of us might like to run a GUI cross-plat, but are not willing to buy into the Xamarin cost of doing business.

Still, epic fail, IMO. Planning is in the wind, discussion concerning ``v2.2`` of Core/Standard. With a weak promise of WinForms/WPF comprehension coming in, let's say, ``v3.x``, to be accurate. Until it happens, this will continue to be the criticism, at least for my part.

Here's a thought: my cringing is related to implicit statefulness.  A classmethod or an instance method carries the risk that a function/method's implementation may not be fully isolated (which I like particularly for testing purposes).  But a staticmethod wouldn't have that problem.  Would using staticmethods for some of these things also fulfill your encapsulation criteria?

The problem with these solutions is that you still don't know the size of the indexing request when you make the call.  Although each document should be roughly the same size on average, occasionally, certain documents are much larger than others. 

Suppose you index 10 docs at a time, and most docs are 100k, but there is a 1% chance that a single doc is 10MB. Then one out of a million requests will be 30MB in size and may make the request fail.

To ensure the bulk index is truly reliable, we need a way to measure the size of the object that we're actually sending, and cut off each request once the total size exceeds a predetermined value. The `bulk_chunks` solution does not do that.

I think `attribute` is handled correctly. At deserialisation, first_name is the source and firstName is the target. In other words, your API exposes `first_name`, not `firstName`. `attribute` is internal implementation detail.

If you want (my guess is you do) to expose `firstName`, you may write 

    firstName = fields.String(attribute='first_name')

and your object will have a `first_name` field after deserialization.

Or use `dump_to` and `load_from`:

    first_name = fields.String(load_from='firstName', dump_to='firstName')

I think the behaviour of those parameters, although documented, can be misleading. I would like to simplify that (see https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/issues/717).

So `attribute` works as expected AFAIU.

`dump_to` / `load_from` are supposed to be handled correctly by apispec as well. I mean they are reflected in the properties in the spec.

Closing for now as the use case you present is correct. Please reopen if you have a failing test case for `dump_to` / `load_from`, or if I misunderstood your point.

Ah, you can safely ignore the warning. It is meant to warn that implicitly-created fields (see https://marshmallow.readthedocs.io/en/latest/quickstart.html#refactoring-implicit-field-creation) set in `Meta.fields` will not be translated into swagger.

`ModelSchema` works a bit differently; marshmallow fields get generated based on `Meta.model` by `ModelSchema's` metaclass *upon class declaration*. Therefore, swagger fields do get generated.

We'll need to find a better condition for emitting the warning. PRs are always welcome =)

The get/set functions for timeout, baudrate etc. were standard in pySerial V1.x and were replaced by properties in V2.x (it was released back in 2003). While V2.x still allowed to call the set/get functions, the `setTimeout` function was not even documented in the V2.x API.

pySerial V3.x supports the V2.x API for backward compatibility but renamed some attributes and functions (though `timeout` was not affected).

So IMHO the other program should be fixed, more than 12 years time to upgrade the API should have been enough ;-)

Alternatively it could depend on pyserial<3 or install a local copy of the `serial` module and point to it using `PYTHONPATH` or similar mechanisms.

Hi

First, a small issue: `ds.map()` always returns a tuple, so you need to unpack it:

```python
    cg_exp = ds.map([np.count_nonzero], axis=0)[0]
```

The reason for this is that you pass in a tuple of functions, and the returned result is a tuple of the result of those functions.

Second, your example is close, but the trick in this case is to scan horizontally, with axis=1, so that scan will return chunks of columns, and then to manually slice the view to get the rows you want:

```python
import loompy
import numpy as np

f = 'my_data.loom'
with loompy.connect(f, 'r') as ds:
    cg_exp = ds.map([np.count_nonzero], axis=0)[0]
    genes = np.where(cg_exp > 2)[0]
    for (ix, selection, view) in ds.scan(axis=1):
        loompy.create_append('gene_filtered_ds.loom', view.layers[genes, :], view.ra[genes], view.ca)
```

Also note that it's always recommended to use a `with` statement, so that the file is automatically closed for you. This prevents many HDF5 errors.

Finally, I always tend to mark genes rather then remove them. So you might do this instead:

```python
import loompy
import numpy as np

f = 'my_data.loom'
with loompy.connect(f, 'r+') as ds:
    cg_exp = ds.map([np.count_nonzero], axis=0)[0]
    ds.ra.Selected = cg_exp > 2
```
(Note the mode must be `r+` since you will be writing to the file). This has the advantage that you will always be able to plot all genes, even if they were not selected. You just need to remember downstream to always filter the genes by the `Selected` attribute. And be careful when you read the attribute - HDF5 doesn't support booleans so it will be returned as an array of 1s and 0s. You need to do `ds.ra.Selected == 1` to get a boolean array back.

I've made the following changes:

* It now doesn't wait until there's a full month's data to download, it'll use whatever it's got.
* It stores all the settlement runs (II, SF, RF).
* There's a global alert (red text on every page) if the importer fails.
* Since the URLs of the current data change on every upload, I've added a feature where the importer scrapes the page for the latest URLs.
* For loading in historical data there's a list of URLs in the configuration that the importer will additionally look at.

So, to install it follow these steps:

* Disable the bsuos importer by setting 'enabled' to 'false'.
* Execute the command: `chellow stop`
* `pip install --upgrade chellow`
* `chellow start`
* Delete all the BSUoS rate scripts except the first one.
* Make sure that the first rate script covers exactly one month.
* Update the BSUoS properties to be:

{
  "discover_urls": true,
  "enabled": true}

* Click the 'import now' button and the latest data should import.
* If you want to import historical data, you can use the URLs of historical data from the National Grid website (https://www.nationalgrid.com/uk/electricity/charging-and-methodology/balancing-services-use-system-bsuos-charges) and put them in the 'urls' list. For example:

{
  "discover_urls": true,
  "enabled": true,
  "urls": ["https://www.nationalgrid.com/sites/default/files/documents/2016-2017%20SF.xls", "https://www.nationalgrid.com/sites/default/files/documents/8589938560-2015-2016%20SF.xlsx"]}

* Remember to remove the items from the 'urls' list after you've imported all the data, otherwise the importer will download them every time.

Just in case: `int2bv` is not always fully interpreted, see also [Z3 questions about int2bv](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/27914582/z3-questions-about-z3-int2bv).

On this particular input, Z3 is very unlucky and it keeps enumerating lots of potential models without finding a good one. If we change the heuristics in some way, it gets lucky again. For instance, with `smt.random_seed=2` it solves it quickly and with `smt.relevancy=0` it solves it even quicker. Another way to approach this is to set `smt.bv.enable_int2bv=false`, which has the effect that int2bv fully uninterpreted and it will report x=0 as a model.

> The Visual Studio backend of cmake produces a solution and project files that aren't usable. They overwhelm VS with a lot of tiny sub-projects. So much so, that it literally takes minutes before it can start a build. 

I noticed the slowness before. I was running Visual Studio in a VM and assumed it was slow because of the VM. When I used it, it was spending most of its time doing the initial indexing I think. It was fine once indexing was complete and Z3 built (and re-built) fine for me.

Are you **sure** that the number of projects here is the bottleneck?

```
git grep z3_add_component | wc -l
76
```

So I'd expect there to be ~76 projects, which doesn't seem that large. Does Visual Studio really not scale well to that number of projects?

> Further, all those little sub-projects have their own compilation settings, so if we need to change a compiler option, we have to either click millions of times, or hack the project files outside of VS.

Err yeah, you shouldn't do that. If you need to change a compiler option you do that at the CMake level. Not the Visual Studio level. When you change the CMake build system the Visual studio solution and projects will get regenerated.

This is the price you pay when you use a meta build system like CMake. Similarly when you use CMake's Makefile backend you don't modify the generated Makefile when you want to change build flags, instead you modify the CMakeLists.txt files or you edit the CMake cache (using `ccmake` or the `cmake-gui` tools).

> The philosophy in VS is a bit different; usually there would be one sub-project for each product, i.e. libz3.dll, z3.exe, c_example, etc, instead of projects that produce static libraries and sub-modules can be within folders or project filters (see currently used .vcxproj files). VS does it's own dependency analysis and incremental compilation features. How much effort is it to teach cmake to do that?

I believe the reason CMake is doing this is because each component is treated as a CMake "object library". CMake "object libraries" act like libraries in CMake's language but they don't get linked, only the object files get built. Then the `libz3` target links all these "object libraries" together to build a monolithic library.

I don't think there's any easy way to fix because I think it might require re-architecting the CMake build. The build is structured the way it is right now because I was trying to make it as similar as possible to the python build system so that they could co-exist easily.

All that being said I really am not an expert on the interaction between Visual Studio and CMake because I don't use it very often.

If it's merely a presentation issue there are ways to reorganise how projects are present in Visual Studio from within CMake

http://blog.audio-tk.com/2015/09/01/sorting-source-files-and-projects-in-folders-with-cmake-and-visual-studioxcode/

https://cmake.org/cmake/help/v3.8/command/source_group.html

If it's more than just a presentation issue then we need to think about how to best fix this. Given that Microsoft's Visual Studio team has recently put a lot of effort into improving the CMake experience in Visual Studio and your connections to Microsoft perhaps it would be good to get in contact with them to see if they can help?

To be honest, I don't think this is the right way to approach this as it would require adding this parameter to all layers with parameters.

The easiest way to only train certain subsets of parameters is to manually construct the list of parameters instead of using `get_all_params()` on the output layer of the network.

Of course this gets a bit tedious. With the new parameter tagging system (see #228), what you could do instead is to mark the parameters you do not wish to train as non-trainable by removing the 'trainable' tag:
```python
layer.params[layer.W].remove('trainable')
```

Then just get the parameters with `get_all_params(..., trainable=True)` to only grab the trainable ones.

Maybe it would also be useful to extend `get_all_params` to allow for specifying layers whose parameters should not be included. But that makes the interface bigger and the code more complicated, and I'm not sure if this use case is common enough for that.

I think the attention network also requires some additional machinery that might not be covered there, because it would need to use the output of two recurrent processes (`h` and `s`) in order to compute the next step in `s`.  E.g.
 1. `h_t = f_1(x_t, h_{t - 1})` (normal recurrent computation)
 1. `c_t = f_2(s_{t - 1}, h)` (depends on `s_{t - 1}` and the entire sequence `h_{1, ... T}`)
 1. `s_t = f_3(c_t, s_{t - 1})` (normal recurrent computation)

@minrk an option might be instead of diffing images or image objects to display both versions side by side (as nbdime can do) in case of conflict. This will give the user a convenient way of comparing them, and addresses a common use case for nbval:

1. Code is written,
2. Notebook is used to create documentation/tutorials for that code.
3. Notebook runs through nbval and 'passes' all the tests (i.e. output cells stored in notebook are consistent with the code that it used to compute that output from the input cells).
4. Code is changed (happens all the time).
5. Notebook runs through nbval and fails some of the tests. 

At this point, it will often be the case that a minor change in the code base results in this fail; this could include a change of colour map and symbol or even updated matplotlib library version. Once the human can see both images, and see that the changes are irrelevant to the role of the test, they can just accept that this 'fail' is not a fail but just needs saving of the updated notebook output.


PS Actually, having written the above, I realise that this may be a bit out of context: I think at the moment, nbval ignores images completely, and it is not even clear we should change this. However, I like the idea of using nbdime to display changes in output cells to allow users to decide whether it is okay to 'update the notebooks' and save the new version in the scenario outline above. So I'll keep that thinking here until we find a better place.

> Why there are test builds against py-moneyed==0.4? Latest version as requirement for django-money is not enough?

Nah, I would believe it's a mistake. The latest releases (or master) of django-money can depend on the latest issues of any dependency as long as it passes tests.

> About rounding. Consider this code:

So the question is: If we store a value with high precision, but we can only ever retrieve it with lower precision, should we store it with this precision, too?

I would definitely say YES. Because if you are accounting money, you always go for rounded numbers. For instance, if currency convertion gives you lots of decimals, you throw them away immediately, otherwise further calculations will be untransparent.

Let's say all our reports and prints say:

1.00
1.00
1.00
1.00
1.00

The sum of that is 5.00

But what if we had this in the database:


1.001
1.001
1.001
1.001
1.001

The sum (using `aggregates.Sum`) of that is 5.005 and would display as 5.01. That result would be impossible to understand and would be very very questionable if used in accounting.

Would you change this to rounding pre-storage? I'm totally :+1: for that!

I suggested that this page on DaVinci/ganga lessons be re-worded a while ago to avoid this sort of confusion in understanding how this should be used.

In short `prepareGaudiExec()` should only be used once (if required) to get your git based project checked out. It was added to address people wanting to run DaVinci jobs without doing local developments. i.e. submitting with new job opts etc.

GaudiExec is designed to work with a local checkout from `lb-dev` somewhere/anywhere. This is required due to the build target for submitting a job to the grid comes from the lhcb scripts.

Also: `help(prepareGaudiExec)` will let you know what arguments you can pass to it to override the defaults which allows for larger scripts to prepare multiple DaVinci areas in different paths.

If you're using the same `build` of DaVinci everywhere I would suggest using `GaudiExec.prepare` to prepare the application once then submit multiple jobs from this prepared application which should be faster as it saves having to checkout and recompile every single time.

To my knowledge (although I'd be grateful if there are any other suggestions) there are three easy-ish ways to allow users to define their own build directories:

**1.** Specifying the BuildPath parameter at build time e.g. MSBuild.exe /p:BuildPath="c:\librealsense2.dll" but I think that only works from the command line
**2.** Edit the csproj and add that same parameter as a property in the csproj e.g. 
```
<PropertyGroup>
    <BuildPath>c:\librealsense2.dll</BuildPath>
 </PropertyGroup>
``` 
That however means changes will be commited.
**3.** Use an environment variable which can be collected by the compiler through a very similar mechanism to the above.

Unfortunately, `A device attached to the system is not functioning` is essentially the generic "something went wrong" error of Media Foundation. We observed this HResult many times in the past, more often on Windows 8 and 7. Now days, Microsoft are making changes to Media Foundation drivers in their rolling updates, so it is critical to know the exact Windows 10 build you are using.

@Goldesel23 so this looks like a slightly modified version of the cpp-headless example. PNG as a file format doesn't usually support 16 bit data (in the case of RealSense depthmaps). There's a function here normalize_depth_to_rgb() which normalizes the 16 bit depth data into an 8 bit 0-255 RGB range, explaining the different colors you see.  

This example was meant to be used for debugging only since the write function is a lossy operation. You can still write 16 bit values into a PNG, but they can't be used for visualization by most image viewing apps.

I have mine broken out into sub "apps", and then I have a utility to "auto load" my queries and migrations.

So, it looks like this:

```
data
    player
        mutations.py
        queries.py
        types.py
    team
        mutations.py
        queries.py
        types.py
    user
        mutations.py
        queries.py
        types.py
    mutation.py
    query.py
```

The contents of `data/query.py` is something like this:

```python
class QueriesAbstract(graphene.ObjectType):
    pass

queries_base_classes = [QueriesAbstract]
current_directory = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))
current_module = current_directory.split('/')[-1]
subdirectories = [
    x
    for x in os.listdir(current_directory)
    if os.path.isdir(os.path.join(current_directory, x)) and
    x != '__pycache__'
]
for directory in subdirectories:
    try:
        module = importlib.import_module(f'{current_module}.{directory}.queries')
        if module:
            classes = [x for x in getmembers(module, isclass)]
            queries = [x[1] for x in classes if 'Query' in x[0]]
            queries_base_classes += queries
    except ModuleNotFoundError:
        pass

queries_base_classes = queries_base_classes[::-1]
properties = {}
for base_class in queries_base_classes:
    properties.update(base_class.__dict__['_meta'].fields)

Queries = type(
    'Queries',
    tuple(queries_base_classes),
    properties
)
```

And, a similar file for `mutation.py`

Probably could be improved some. But, what this allows.. me to do:

```python
schema = Schema(query=query.Queries, mutation=mutation.Mutations)
```

And, all I need to do is create a file in the right directory and everything gets loaded automatically.

When used with PyPy the difference is even bigger, and this is just the beginning.
Also, when having multiple fields in a same ObjectType, the improvement significant.

After finishing this promise implementation, I will work on separate the serializer that I assume will give another ~2x gains if using a simple `dict` instead of `OrderedDict` for serialization, and maybe even higher if serialized directly to `JSON`. This will also open the possibility of using other serializers like `msgpack` :)

And after that, optimizations with `Cython` will help to crush all benchmarks! 😊

After further analyzing the issue, I think @hirokiky original suggested fix is the best one. I think it is incorrect for `Panel.process_resposne()` to return a non-rendered response. Unlike Django middleware, panels do not have the (`process-template-response`)[https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/topics/http/middleware/#process-template-response] method to ensure that the response is eventually rendered. As I don't see a use case for panels returning non-rendered response, I don't think there is a need ot add one.

So @njarboe there already is something similar to magic_write implemented in the 3.0 data model in PmagPy. However, as during the 3.0 conversion things have gone more OOP it is in the MagicDataFrame object that @moonshoes87 created which is generally accessed wrapped in a Contribution object (also @moonshoes87's work) which has a number of methods for propagating data between tables and such. So you would theoretically in a full 3.0 converter you would:

1. create a Pandas DataFrame for each table
2. fill each table
3. instantiate a Contribution object with the correct tables (i.e. con = Contribution(stuff))
4. insert your filled DataFrames in the MagicDataFrames contained in the Contribution tables (i.e. con.tables['measurements'].df = meas_df)
5. finally you can call MagicDataFrame.write_magic_file (con.tables['measurements'].write_magic_file())

The other pmag.py functions that handle say sample name to site name conversion and such should all still work as they are data model independent. If you want to see the code and try creating this 3.0 native converter these objects are defined in new_builder.py.

Regarding python 3.3 and the change in c4ff3cc : 

`SSLContext.check_hostname` changes the behavior of `SSLSocket.do_handshake()`, and causes it to automatically perform  call to `match_hostname()` during the handshake.

Anymail by default* generates a new message_id for each message it sends through SendGrid. It sets that in the message's Message-ID header, which is what SendGrid is supposed to pass back to webhook calls as the smtp-id field.

Two questions:

1. Can you check an actual sent message as received in your inbox, and see what Message-ID header it ended up with? An Anymail-generated Message-ID should look like `<numbers@your-from-email-domain>`. If it does, but you're getting a different smtp-id in the webhook, that sounds like a SendGrid bug. If it doesn't, then SendGrid has overridden the Message-ID header from Anymail, and we'd need to figure out why. (Also, there should be exactly one Message-ID/Message-Id header, case-insensitive.)

2. Your example code doesn't provide a `from_email` for the `msg`, so Django's DEFAULT_FROM_EMAIL setting will be used. Have you set that to something that matches a domain you've authenticated with SendGrid? (Related: when you say you "receive an smtp-id of `<20180523175141.98405.51520@example.com>` in the msg.anymail_status", the "example.com" part was really your actual sending domain, and you just redacted it here, right?)

I'll try to check later to see if SendGrid changed something out from under us, but my best guess right now is they're maybe overriding Message-ID with their own value under some undocumented conditions.

\* I'm assuming you've already seen Anymail's [docs on SendGrid Message-IDs](https://anymail.readthedocs.io/en/stable/esps/sendgrid/#sendgrid-message-id), and that your settings don't override Anymail's default SENDGRID_GENERATE_MESSAGE_ID=True.

Hi, this issue is actually due to `None` not being a valid value for `--installer`, although it's listed as a default value in our command line help text:
```
  -i INSTALLER, --installer INSTALLER
                        Installer plugin name (also used to find domains).
                        (default: None)
```

Previously the value of `--installer` wasn't used for renew functionality, hence the value was never checked during runtime for `certbot renew`, this however changed with the recent Certbot versions when we added functionality to check if a requested installer needs to perform tasks on renewal.

If you initially acquired your certificates without installer (ie. running `certbot certonly ...`) the `certbot renew` verb without `--installer` defined should skip this part altogether. The idea was that a `CombinedParameter`  is `Parameter`, meaning that it is getable, setable and supports usage in the loop. In the current implementation it only supports use in the Loop through a set_array. 

The underlying problem this relates to, and that the proposed implementation would solve, was that of "tuple-like" parameters, simply put parameters that have multiple units and labels. >A Parameter represents a state variable of your system. Parameters may be settable, gettable, or both. While many Parameters represent a setting or measurement for a particular Instrument, it is possible to define Parameters that represent more powerful abstractions.

Before we move forward, there is another point that I'd like to raise.
With the basic fallback implementation that I mentioned in the original post (`StandardTransform`), we can have the sequential and individual operations that are currently supported by the datasets.

For more complex cases where we want to apply joint transforms but also individual transforms (for example in segmentation, we perform colour augmentation in the input image, and random crops / flips in the input and target), I think the way to go would be to encourage the user to write their own `Transform` class, which leverages other `Transform` classes, and implement the `__call__` that chains the operations together, like in pytorch, instead of trying to use the `Compose` transform to write complicated transformations. Something like
```python
class MySegmentationTransform(object):

    def __init__(self, input_color_transform,
                 joint_random_crop_transform, joint_random_flip_transform):
        self.input_color_transform = input_color_transform
        self.joint_random_crop_transform = joint_random_crop_transform
        self.joint_random_flip_transform = joint_random_flip_transform

    def __call__(self, input, target):
        input, target = self.joint_random_crop_transform(input, target)
        input, target = self.joint_random_flip_transform(input, target)
        input = convert_pil_to_tensor(input)
        target = convert_pil_to_tensor(target)
        input = self.input_color_transform(input) / 255
        return input, target
```

Unfortunately this PR got a little out of hand - I ran into two separate issues while trying to fix the original issue that prompted this PR, so let me talk about what's going on here.

# https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/pull/1725/commits/dced570e92596c84a8337ab957284c9243609ebd 
This fixes a bug (https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/issues/1726) that has been lurking in our system for some time. It was exacerbated by the tests in `lifecycle_test.go` being fast and parallelism. The root cause is that we signal to the resource monitor that a step has completed executing *before* we have committed the results of the step to the snapshot. If we get very unlucky with the Go runtime scheduler, it is possible for a worker goroutine to signal that it is done with a step and get zero CPU time until a dependent step has already dispatched, completed, and committed. This manifests as resources appearing in the wrong order in the snapshot.

To fix this problem, I had `Step.Apply` return a function that should be run by the step executor when that step has completely finished processing. (I hate re-using words, but "retired" is apt, since the side effects of the step become visible to the rest of the system.) For steps that require it, this function will signal to the resource monitor that the step has completed executing.

This is related to the original PR because the refresh source iterator is using this channel to determine when it is OK to send the next source event.

## https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/pull/1725/commits/afca7696a1deb241b2919665a71650c4c618a2f1

This commit fixes an issue (I didn't make an issue to track this because it's pretty small) that was also lurking but I encountered for the first time. If the final step in a plan is a `Replace` step, we accidentally failed to mark the snapshot as valid again and any future updates would fail. The fix here is to do an "empty" mutation when a `Replace` step completes so that the snapshot is re-validated. Note that this code is basically moot because I will be replacing this code within a day or so (https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/issues/1626).

Thanks for reporting the issue.

Currently we ship our own version of pyarrow with Ray. When you call `import ray`, we modify the python path so that `import pyarrow` finds our version of pyarrow. See

https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/c21e18937137a17205d7ee0aa6158e7058ef6796/python/ray/__init__.py#L9-L11

When you do `import pyarrow` before `import ray`, you are actually getting a different version of pyarrow. At the moment, pyarrow is undergoing a lot of development and so we rely on using pyarrow at very specific commits. Down the road we'll just install pyarrow through pip and not ship it ourselves.

Maybe what we should do here (to prevent the crash) is to check if pyarrow has already been imported and raise an exception if so (when ray is imported).

Regarding the whole release automation thing, I think I've convinced myself that the main issues to worry about are:

* New maintainers: if we do release-after-merge, that makes it scarier to click the "merge" button
* Users: don't want to wait for new features, but also probably would rather not be reading release notes every day to find out what's been deprecated now

Brainstorming about how to address these, I came up with two ideas:

Option 1: implement release-after-merge, and figure out how to make it work

For new maintainers: I think the crucial thing is to have some way to feel like if you did make some major error, there's some chance for someone else to catch it -- but without actually requiring "someone else" to sign off. Maybe if there were a way to merge on a timer? Like we could have a bot where if someone with commit access marks a PR as "approved", and after 24 hours no-one has "requested changes", then the bot goes ahead and merges it.

For users: So it's probably a good idea in any case to pin your trio version in your project, and then use a service like [pyup.io](https://pyup.io) to send a PR when a new release comes out. And if you're using pyup.io, then you can pick how often it sends these updates, e.g. you can tell it that no matter how often someone releases, it should only nag you once a week. **But**... as @pquentin pointed out to me, using pyup.io inside companies may be impossible because for private repos it costs money and many companies are dysfunctional about this kind of thing. On the other hand, maybe those companies are already dysfunctional about upgrades and only upgrade once a year or something, I don't know?

Option 2: Do automated releases on a fixed schedule, e.g. once a week. (Unless there's a bug labeled "release blocker", I guess, which should hopefully never happen, but maybe you want the option.)

I can't decide if this idea is super clever or would just be kind of annoying :-). It keeps that key "releases just magically happen" flavor, so you're still forced to figure out how to automate things, people can count on timely updates, etc. But for maintainers, it breaks that feeling that every time you hit merge you're immediately pushing code out to the world, and 

----

There's also some question about what to do with all the other python-trio projects. For the ones that only get small and occasional changes (like `async_generator`, `sphinxcontrib-trio`, ...), the hypothesis approach makes a *lot* of sense. Maybe we go ahead and implement it there and see how it goes before committing to anything on the main trio project?

There's also still the concern about vandalism, but it's so vanishingly rare that maybe we should wait for it to actually happen before worrying about it.

(And again, would love to hear feedback from other maintainers and users, because really I'm just guessing about what would help you all :-).)

The only thing a nursery remembers about old children is if they raised an
exception, in which case it remembers the exception while it's waiting for
all the other children to finish shutting down. So nursery memory
consumption should be proportional to the number of currently running tasks
(and barely even that). If you ever see something different then that's a
bug.

How are you testing the memory?  There's no guarantee the OS will reclaim the memory once the Jep instance is closed, hence I put it in a for loop where it eventually plateaus.

The change is `Jep.close()` loops over the PyObjects to close them, and `PyObject.close()` calls `PyObject.decref()`.  `PyObject.decref()` then calls `isValid()` and `isValid()` fails because it calls `jep.isValidThread()` which fails because jep.closed was set to true.  So we need to ensure closed on the Jep instance is set to true after the PyObjects are closed.

# Context

After some discussion with @lgarithm, we came up with a conceptual design that would highly clarify the TL architecture and make future development a lot more easy and integrable.

@lgarithm: Please edit my post if you want to add new stuff or correct some stuff I m ight have got wrong.

#Conceptual Design

Layer and Network Classes will be **converted** to factory-like objects. They contain critical information to generate Layers and Network, however, they do not contain anymore any information about the generated Layer. 

## Layer API
Layer Class becomes a factory encapsulating the required information to generate a TF Ops, TF Tensors and TF Vars. Layer object do not contain anymore any information about the network or TF objects.

### Deprecation 

Which means that these informations will be removed:
```python
Layer.outputs
Layer.inputs
Layer.all_params
Layer.all_layers
Layer.all_graph
and so on ...
```

### Factory API

In order to insure a stable and consistent API, connecting Layer will now happen using the functional API. This manner to call Layers allows us to use Layer as factories without having to give all the parameters again.

```python
tl.layers.Conv2D(prev_layer, ...)  # Deprecated
tl.layers.Conv2D(...)(prev_layer)  # Using the Layer.__call__() method, factory way.
```

### Layer Output

Now a TL Layer returns a TF Tensor, not a TL Layer anymore.

## Network API
Network Classes (CustomNetwork and Sequential) also become factories encapsulating the required information to generate the Model using TL Layers.

### Deprecation 

Which means that these informations will be removed:
```python
Network.outputs
Network.inputs
Network.all_params
Network.all_layers
Network.all_graph
and so on ...
```

### Factory API

The model generation will be launched using the `.compile()` method.

Network.compile(input_placeholder, is_train=True, reuse=True)

### Network Output

The model output a new class `CompiledNetwork`, this model will have all the old attributes and methods that makes TL Layers so easy and pleasant to use.
